title: SACRAMENTO -

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "SB 27 will expand California’s carbon sequestration capabilities and improve the carbon retention of the state’s natural and working lands.",
    "Direct Air Capture technology is being explored to mimic natural carbon sequestration processes.",
    "Scientists on the United Nation’s Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change have concluded that stopping the worst impacts of climate change will require deployment of technologies that can directly remove greenhouse gases from the air.",
    "Effective natural carbon removal solutions, also known as carbon sequestration, exist today.",
    "A report from the Lawrence Livermore National Labs concluded that carbon sequestration is among the least expensive ways to mitigate climate change.",
    "Land-based carbon retention and capture practices like healthy soils agricultural practices, rangeland improvements, wetlands restoration, and sustainable forestry use nature’s systems to draw carbon out of the atmosphere.",
    "Carbon sequestration on natural and working lands is relatively inexpensive – about $11 per ton of CO2 removed, on average.",
    "Costs for newer technologies like direct air capture are coming down fast.",
    "Linking quality carbon sequestration with companies who are investing in carbon neutrality presents a unique opportunity to fund the fight against climate change.",
    "SB 27 will require the Natural Resources Agency to establish carbon sequestration goals for natural and working lands by July 2023."
  ]
}
```
